items.title: Announcing the AI Gateway Working Group
items.summary: <p>The community around Kubernetes includes a number of Special Interest Groups (SIGs) and Working Groups (WGs) facilitating discussions on important topics between interested contributors. Today, we're excited to announce the formation of the <a href="https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/wg-ai-
items.reason: Kubernetes AI Working Group is a new governance topic that is not covered in the current wiki; creates a distinct article about AI‑related SIG/WG in Kubernetes.

items.title: Before You Migrate: Five Surprising Ingress-NGINX Behaviors You Need to Know
items.summary:
<p>As <a href="https://kubernetes.io/blog/2025/11/11/ingress-nginx-retirement/">announced</a> November 2025, Kubernetes will retire Ingress-NGINX in March 2026.
Despite its widespread usage, Ingress-NGINX is full of surprising defaults and side effects that are probably present in your clus
items.reason: Detailed analysis of Ingress‑NGINX retirement and hidden behaviours is highly relevant for Kubernetes users; no existing entry covers this upcoming change.

items.title: Flexibility Over Lock-In: The Enterprise Shift in Agent Strategy
items.summary: Building agents is now a strategic priority for 95% of respondents in our latest State of Agentic AI research, which surveyed more than 800 developers and decision-makers worldwide. The shift is happening quickly: agent adoption has moved beyond experiments and demos into early operational maturity.
items.reason: State‑of‑the‑art survey on enterprise agent strategy introduces a new perspective on AI agents that the wiki does not yet document.

items.title: Building AI Teams: How Docker Sandboxes and Docker Agent Transform Development
items.summary: It's 11 PM. You've got a JIRA ticket open, an IDE with three unsaved files, a browser tab on Stack Overflow, and another on documentation. You're context-switching between designing UI, writing backend APIs, fixing bugs, and running tests. You're wearing all the hats, product manager, designer, engi
items.reason: Practical guide on building AI teams with Docker Sandboxes and Docker Agent adds a unique workflow topic absent from the wiki.

items.title: What’s Holding Back AI Agents? It’s Still Security
items.summary: It’s hard to find a team today that isn’t talking about agents. For most organizations, this isn’t a “someday” project anymore. Building agents is a strategic priority for 95% of respondents that we surveyed across the globe with 800+ developers and decision makers in our latest State of Agentic AI
items.reason: Security challenges for AI agents are a distinct, emerging concern; the wiki lacks a dedicated security‑focused article.

items.title: Celebrating Women in AI: 3 Questions with Cecilia Liu on Leading Docker’s MCP Strategy
items.summary: To celebrate International Women’s Day, we sat down with Cecilia Liu, Senior Product Manager at Docker, for three questions about the vision and strategy behind Docker’s MCP solutions. From shaping product direction to driving AI innovation, Cecilia plays a key role in defining how Docker enables se
items.reason: Interview with a Docker leader on women in AI provides valuable community and leadership insight not yet covered.

items.title: Announcing Docker Hardened System Packages
items.summary: Your Package Manager, Now with a Security Upgrade Last December, we made Docker Hardened Images (DHI) free because we believe secure, minimal, production-ready images should be the default. Every developer deserves strong security at no cost. It should not be complicated or locked behind a paywall.
items.reason: Docker Hardened System Packages introduce a new security offering; warrants its own documentation page.

items.title: Continuous AI for accessibility: How GitHub transforms feedback into inclusion
items.summary:
AI automates triage for accessibility feedback, allowing us to focus on fixing barriers—turning a chaotic backlog into continuous, rapid resolutions.
The post Continuous AI for accessibility: How GitHub transforms feedback into inclusion appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: GitHub’s continuous AI for accessibility showcases a novel AI‑driven workflow; no existing wiki entry covers this use‑case.

items.title: GitHub availability report: February 2026
items.summary:
In February, we experienced six incidents that resulted in degraded performance across GitHub services.
The post GitHub availability report: February 2026 appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: Monthly availability report is a recurring operational metric; creating a template page for GitHub availability history is useful.

items.title: Addressing GitHub’s recent availability issues
items.summary:
GitHub recently experienced several availability incidents. We understand the impact these outages have on our customers and are sharing details on the stabilization work we’re prioritizing right now.
The post Addressing GitHub’s recent availability issues appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: Post‑mortem on recent GitHub outages adds to the incident‑response knowledge base, which is currently missing.

items.title: The era of “AI as text” is over. Execution is the new interface.
items.summary:
AI is shifting from prompt-response interactions to programmable execution. See how the GitHub Copilot SDK enables agentic workflows directly inside your applications.
The post The era of “AI as text” is over. Execution is the new interface. appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: Shift from “AI as text” to execution‑centric interfaces is a major trend; requires a dedicated article on agentic workflows and Copilot SDK.

items.title: Under the hood: Security architecture of GitHub Agentic Workflows
items.summary:
GitHub Agentic Workflows are built with isolation, constrained outputs, and comprehensive logging. Learn how our threat model and security architecture help teams run agents safely in GitHub Actions. 
The post Under the hood: Security architecture of GitHub Agentic Workflows appeared first on The Gi
items.reason: Technical deep‑dive into GitHub Agentic Workflows security architecture fills a gap in the wiki’s security section.
